title: Vegetarian mulligatawny
subtitle: Anglo-Indian lentil and vegetable pepper-water soup
excerpt: A thick, warming soup of red lentils, carrot, and apple in a spiced onion base, finished with coconut milk and a squeeze of lemon. The Anglo-Indian adaptation of the Tamil milagu tannir (pepper water), transformed by colonial kitchens into a hearty meal starter.
sourceNotes: Based on Anglo-Indian mulligatawny as documented in Lizzie Collingham, 'Curry: A Tale of Cooks and Conquerors' (2005) and Madhur Jaffrey, 'A Taste of India' (1985). The vegetarian version follows the soup's older form more closely than the later meat-enriched variants, using lentils as the primary protein and retaining the sourness that characterises the Tamil original.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze before adding the coconut milk. Add it when reheating.
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles well in a larger pan.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps refrigerated for 3 days. Add the coconut milk and lemon when reheating.

content.text: Mulligatawny began as milagu tannir (pepper water), a thin, sour, deeply spiced broth from Tamil Nadu that was used as a digestive and condiment. British colonial officers adopted it, thickened it with lentils and meat, sweetened it with apple and onion, and sent it home to England as the defining soup of the Anglo-Indian table. By the nineteenth century it appeared in every British cookery book and club menu, often bearing only a distant resemblance to the original.

This version is vegetarian and leans closer to the soup's southern Indian origins: red lentils for body, a spiced base of onion, carrot, and apple, coconut milk for richness, and enough acidity from lemon to sharpen the finish. It is faster and lighter than the chicken or lamb variants and works equally well as a starter or a bowl with bread.

items.prepNote: peeled and diced; Bramley or any tart apple

content.text: Heat {{sunflower-oil}} of oil in a large saucepan over a medium heat. Add {{onion}} diced onion and cook for 8 minutes until softened and lightly golden. Add {{garlic}} crushed garlic, {{ginger-fresh}} grated ginger, {{curry-powder}} of curry powder, and {{turmeric-ground}} of turmeric. Stir for 2 minutes until fragrant.

Add {{carrot}} diced carrot, {{cooking-apple}} diced apple, and {{red-lentils}} of rinsed red lentils. Pour in {{vegetable-stock}} of vegetable stock and season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Bring to the boil, reduce the heat, and simmer for 20–25 minutes until the lentils and vegetables are completely soft. Stir in {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk and {{lemon}} of lemon juice. Taste and adjust the seasoning. Serve hot — the soup should be thick but pourable; add a little more stock if needed.

items.cause: Lentils have absorbed all the stock
items.fix: Add more stock or boiling water and stir to combine. Mulligatawny should be thick but flowing, not a porridge.
Add more lemon juice — the soup needs real sharpness to read correctly. Taste after adding more salt as well, as both work together.

items.cause: Curry powder too hot for the amount used
items.fix: The coconut milk softens heat. Start with 1 tablespoon of curry powder if using a hot blend, and add the second tablespoon only after tasting.

content.text: Mulligatawny was the great Anglo-Indian import to the Victorian table and the first curry many British people ever tasted, encountered in club dining rooms and middle-class households throughout the nineteenth century. It appears in every major Victorian cookery manual, from Mrs Beeton onwards, usually with a meat component added. The vegetarian version reclaims something of the original Tamil pepper-water tradition, which was meatless and intended as a digestive, before it was enriched and Anglicised into the thick soup familiar today.
